headline: Drawing Goals: How Football Opens Doors to Mental Health
meta_description: Saudi Arabia-based organization uses football and art to help young men discuss mental health, breaking stigma through a universal language.
summary_short: An innovative approach in Saudi Arabia combines art and football to help young men overcome barriers to discussing mental health.
body:
In Saudi Arabia, Dr. Sahira Al Nahari discovered something remarkable: when young men arrived at her therapeutic art workshops feeling guarded about their emotions, a simple prompt changed everything. Sketching their favourite football teams became the gateway to conversations about everyday anxieties—proof that sport can unlock doors traditional mental health discussions cannot.

Shifā Art, the organization Dr. Al Nahari founded, recognizes what football fans intuitively know: no player wins alone. "Players depend on a whole ecosystem—defenders, midfielders, coaches, medics and fans in the stands," she explained. That same principle applies to youth mental health, which requires community support rather than clinical responses in isolation.

The timing is significant. As one in seven young people aged 10 to 19 experience mental health conditions, and 75 per cent of adult mental health conditions emerge by the mid-twenties, the UN is championing football as a proven intervention. Research shows team sports correlate with lower depression and anxiety rates across countries.

On Friday, the UN Youth Office hosted leaders and athletes at a "One World, One Game, One Goal" event to celebrate football's transformative potential. Yet both Dr. Al Nahari and UN Assistant Secretary-General Felipe Paullier emphasized this is just one piece of a larger puzzle. Most countries allocate less than two per cent of health budgets to mental health—leaving millions of young people carrying unseen, unspoken pain that demands multisectoral solutions.
